content:
With the mechanistic framework of damp heat aging now established, the next critical question concerns the quantitative kinetics of adhesion degradation at specific encapsulant interfaces—how rapidly and to what extent does interfacial adhesion decline under accelerated aging, and do different interfaces within the same module degrade at comparable rates?

Long-duration accelerated aging provides the most direct evidence of adhesion degradation kinetics. At 10,000 hours of accelerated aging, EVA adhesion within mini-modules falls to a level generally seen after more than 15 years of field exposure [REF:6]. This benchmark establishes that the standard IEC damp heat test duration of 1,000–2,000 hours captures only a fraction of the total adhesion loss that accumulates over a module's operational lifetime, and that extended accelerated aging protocols are necessary to probe the long-term adhesion envelope. The 10,000-hour threshold represents a particularly informative data point because it maps accelerated laboratory conditions onto real-world field degradation timescales, providing module designers with a quantitative basis for predicting when adhesion may approach critically low levels [REF:6].

A key finding from interfacial analysis is that degradation rates are not uniform across all layer boundaries within a module. The glass/EVA interface experiences more degradation than the EVA/cell interface, explaining why this initially stronger interface becomes the preferential site for delamination [REF:5]. This differential degradation is mechanistically significant: the glass/EVA interface relies on silane coupling agent bonds that are susceptible to hydrolytic attack, whereas the EVA/cell interface involves different adhesion chemistry. The fact that the initially stronger glass/EVA bond eventually becomes the weaker link under damp heat exposure [REF:5] implies that adhesion measurements taken immediately after lamination do not reliably predict which interface will fail first during service. This inversion of relative interface strength during aging has direct implications for quality assurance protocols, which currently focus on as-laminated peel strength without accounting for differential aging rates.

The substrate type itself plays a statistically significant role in determining adhesion degradation outcomes. Three-way ANOVA analysis revealed that both substrate type (p=0.00) and aging method (p=0.00) had significant effects on bond strength, whereas the adhesive approach (self-etch versus etch-and-rinse) did not affect bond strength (p=0.84) [REF:8]. This statistical finding, though derived from dental adhesive substrates, carries an important transferable insight: the intrinsic properties of the substrate surface dominate adhesion retention under aging, outweighing differences in surface preparation methodology. For encapsulant systems, this suggests that the glass surface chemistry and cell metallization composition may be more critical determinants of long-term adhesion than variations in the application protocol itself [REF:8].

Complementary screening approaches provide additional context for understanding adhesion degradation across different material systems. Nine candidate experimental adhesives were screened for physical properties including degree of conversion, surface microhardness, surface water contact angle, and microtensile bond strength, followed by either thermocycling at 10,000 cycles between 5–50 degrees Celsius or 1 year of biochemical aging in simulated media [REF:4]. The multi-property screening methodology employed in this work [REF:4] illustrates the value of evaluating adhesion in conjunction with other physical parameters (hardness, contact angle, degree of cure) rather than relying on peel strength alone. Degradation in micro-tensile bond strength correlated with changes in surface hardness and water contact angle, suggesting that adhesion loss is one manifestation of broader polymer network degradation that is detectable through multiple characterization methods [REF:4].

The durability of adhesive bonds on construction substrates, evaluated through peel adhesion testing of self-adhesive tapes before and after artificial aging [REF:7], provides a useful cross-domain comparison. While the substrate and adhesive chemistries differ substantially from those in photovoltaic or flexible electronics encapsulation, the methodological approach —measuring peel adhesion retention as a function of aging exposure on different substrate types—is directly analogous. The observation that surface characteristics of the substrate influence adhesion durability [REF:7][REF:8] converges with findings from encapsulant studies showing that the glass/EVA interface degrades preferentially over the EVA/cell interface [REF:5], reinforcing the principle that substrate-dependent interfacial chemistry governs degradation kinetics across diverse adhesive systems.

| Parameter | Glass/EVA Interface | EVA/Cell Interface |
|---|---|---|
| Relative initial strength | Higher | Lower |
| Degradation rate under DH | Higher [REF:5] | Lower [REF:5] |
| Preferential failure site | Yes, after aging [REF:5] | No |
| Key aging milestone | 10,000h = 15+ yr field [REF:6] | Not characterized |
| Dominant factor in retention | Substrate type (p=0.00) [REF:8] | — |

Several limitations constrain the generalizability of these kinetic findings to DVS-PEI encapsulants in flexible electronics. The 10,000-hour aging benchmark [REF:6] and the differential interface degradation data [REF:5] were obtained from glass-frontsheet PV modules using EVA encapsulant—a thermoset with vinyl acetate chemistry distinct from the polyetherimide backbone of DVS-PEI. The ANOVA findings on substrate dominance [REF:8] were derived from dental adhesive systems with dentin and enamel substrates rather than polymer or glass substrates relevant to electronics. The convergence of substrate-dependent degradation across PV modules [REF:5], dental adhesives [REF:8], and construction tapes [REF:7] provides indirect support for the hypothesis that similar interfacial chemistry principles would apply to DVS-PEI systems, but direct experimental verification is absent.

Future research should prioritize quantitative peel force and fracture energy measurements at DVS-PEI/glass and DVS-PEI/flexible substrate interfaces as a function of DH aging duration. The establishment of field-equivalent aging milestones for DVS-PEI, analogous to the 10,000-hour EVA benchmark [REF:6], would provide critical design guidance. Additionally, determining whether DVS-PEI exhibits the same inversion of interface strength hierarchy during aging as EVA [REF:5] would inform substrate preparation and quality assurance strategies specific to flexible electronics applications.

**Key Findings**

- At 10,000 hours of accelerated aging, EVA adhesion in mini-modules falls to a level equivalent to more than 15 years of field exposure, establishing a quantitative aging-to-field correlation [REF:6].
- The glass/EVA interface degrades preferentially over the EVA/cell interface under damp heat, causing an inversion of relative interface strength during aging [REF:5].
- Substrate type and aging method are the dominant factors affecting bond strength retention (both p=0.00), whereas adhesive application approach has no significant effect (p=0.84) [REF:8].
- Multi-property screening of adhesive candidates reveals that adhesion loss correlates with changes in surface hardness and water contact angle after thermocycling [REF:4].
- Cross-domain evidence from construction tape adhesion [REF:7] converges with PV and dental findings [REF:5][REF:8] in demonstrating that substrate surface chemistry dominates adhesion durability under aging.
